planting.note: Plant in spring once all frost risk has passed and soil has warmed. Choose a sturdy trellis, fence, or tree to support the hooked stems, and site in full sun with fertile, well-drained soil. Mulch generously around the base to retain moisture through the warm growing season.

directions: Take semi-hardwood cuttings 4-6 inches long in late spring or summer, remove lower leaves, dip in rooting hormone, and insert into a warm, humid propagation environment with bottom heat near 80°F. Once well rooted, harden off and transplant to a permanent location with robust support, as the vine climbs by means of strong hooked stems that anchor to host structures. Water consistently during establishment, then reduce frequency once the plant is settled; mature plants tolerate brief dry spells but flower most profusely with regular irrigation and monthly balanced fertilization from spring through fall. Prune lightly after each flush of bloom to encourage branching and additional flowering, and position near seating areas or windows to take full advantage of the powerful ylang-ylang fragrance that intensifies in the evening.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost in a location with full sun and rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil. In zone 9, choose a warm microclimate sheltered from cold north winds, and mulch deeply to protect roots from any winter cold snaps.

directions: Propagate from semi-hardwood cuttings taken in summer, treating the basal cut with indole-butyric acid rooting hormone and maintaining high humidity and bottom heat of 75-85°F until rooting occurs in 6-10 weeks. Transplant rooted cuttings into fertile, well-drained, humus-rich soil in a warm, protected site and water thoroughly to settle the roots. Fertilize with a balanced slow-release fertilizer in spring and again in early summer, supplementing with a micronutrient drench if foliage yellows on alkaline soils. The intensely fragrant pure-white flowers are produced year-round in the warmest climates and peak in spring and summer; harvest individual blooms in the early morning for use in garlands or hair ornaments, as the fragrance is most potent just after opening.

common_name: Coco Magnolia / Dwarf White Jade Orchid

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot with bright filtered light or morning sun and afternoon shade. Magnolia coco prefers moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil and performs exceptionally well in large containers, which allow the root zone to be protected during unusual cold spells in zone 9.

directions: Take semi-hardwood cuttings in late spring or early summer, wound the base lightly, apply rooting hormone, and root under intermittent mist or inside a humidity tent at 75-80°F with bright indirect light. Transplant rooted cuttings into organically enriched, well-drained soil and avoid heavy clay or sites prone to waterlogging, as Magnolia coco is sensitive to root rot. Unlike most magnolias that bloom in a single dramatic flush, this compact species produces its nodding, creamy-white, intensely sweet-scented flowers from spring through fall, making it especially rewarding near a patio, entryway, or courtyard window. Fertilize with an acid-forming fertilizer in early spring and midsummer, and deadhead spent flowers to keep the plant tidy and encourage the next wave of bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in spring into prepared soil or deep containers, as Polyalthia longifolia does not transplant well once established and is best started in situ or in deep root-trainer pots. Site in full sun with deep, well-drained soil and adequate overhead clearance for the tree's eventual 30-60 foot height.

directions: Sow fresh seed immediately after harvest, as viability declines rapidly; plant one to two seeds per deep root-trainer pot or directly in the ground at a depth of 0.5-1 inch, covering lightly with moist soil. Germination typically occurs within 2-4 weeks in warm, humid conditions above 72°F, and seedlings should be watered consistently but not waterlogged during the first growing season. The Indian mast tree is prized for its dramatically columnar, weeping habit and its tolerance of heat, drought, and urban pollution once established, making it an outstanding choice for avenue planting, windbreaks, or as a vertical accent in tropical landscapes. Feed young trees monthly with a balanced fertilizer through the first two years to encourage rapid development of the characteristic tall, narrow silhouette.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in spring in warm, humid conditions. Choose a site with partial shade and moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; Polyalthia suberosa naturally grows as an understory shrub and performs best with some protection from intense afternoon sun in zone 10-11 tropical gardens.

directions: Sow fresh seed at a depth of 0.5 inch in moist, well-drained potting mix and maintain temperatures above 72°F to achieve germination within 2-4 weeks; allow seedlings to reach 6-8 inches before transplanting to a permanent understory or semi-shaded garden position. Corky bark polyalthia establishes most successfully in organically enriched, consistently moist soil with good drainage, and benefits from a layer of organic mulch to retain moisture and suppress weeds during the first growing season. Once established, this unusual custard-apple family shrub requires minimal maintenance; its small cream to yellow-green flowers attract pollinators and are followed by small black fruits that draw birds, adding wildlife interest to tropical garden compositions. The deeply furrowed, corky gray-brown bark develops distinctive textural character with age, making it a conversation piece as a specimen shrub or informal screen in zone 10-11 tropical and subtropical gardens.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System
